paper.title: New Trends in Treating Cushing's Disease

entities.name: New Trends in Treating Cushing's Disease

evidence_entities.text: Rates of recurrence and/or persistence of Cushing's disease after surgical treatment are high. Recently, advances in molecular insights and a better understanding of pathophysiology have enabled the development of potential therapeutic targets that could control adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) and cortisol secretion or even reduce tumour cell proliferation.

Cushing's disease (CD) is caused by an adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH)-secreting pituitary adenoma, or rarely carcinoma, and is considered a highly morbid endocrine disorder with few medical options.
Pasireotide is a novel somatostatin analogue targeting four out of five SSTR subtypes, with the highest affinity for SSTR5.
Levoketoconazole normalized mean urinary free cortisol (mUFC) levels in more than 50% of the patients.
Osilodrostat resulted in a complete response in 66% and a partial response (≥50% reduction of UFC) in 9% of the enrolled patients.
Relacorilant does not bind to the progesterone receptor, rendering patients free of progesterone receptor modulator-associated endometrial changes.

notes: This paper is a comprehensive review of new therapeutic approaches for Cushing's disease, focusing on novel drugs targeting different mechanisms including pituitary-directed agents, steroidogenesis inhibitors, and immunotherapy approaches.
The paper discusses both approved drugs (pasireotide, levoketoconazole, osilodrostat) and experimental treatments in various stages of clinical development.
Multiple clinical trials are referenced throughout the paper, indicating active research in this therapeutic area.
